title: Making a simple leather belt
subtitle: Cut, skive the ends, bevel the edges, and fit the buckle
excerpt: How to make a plain leather belt from a strap of vegetable-tanned leather: cutting to width, tapering and skiving the buckle end, bevelling and burnishing the edges, punching the holes, and fitting the buckle.
sourceNotes: Traditional leatherwork and saddlery guides. Alex Warneke Leathercraft Manual. Bruce Grant Encyclopaedia of Rawhide and Leather Braiding (public domain techniques).

content.text: A plain belt is an excellent first leatherwork project: it requires only hand tools, produces a functional result, and introduces the core techniques of cutting, edge work, and burnishing. The leather to use is
vegetable-tanned leather
at 3 to 4 mm thickness. Pre-cut belt blanks are available in standard widths (25, 30, 38 mm) which saves the cutting step.
What you need
Leather strap or belt blank.
Measure from the centre hole of your existing belt to the buckle pin, then add 300 mm for the buckle fold and tail. 3 to 4 mm thickness, 25 to 38 mm wide.
Buckle.
A centre-bar or roller buckle. The bar width must match the belt width.
Drive punch or revolving punch.
For cutting the wear holes in the tip end and the buckle-pin hole. A 4 mm or 5 mm punch makes a standard belt hole.
Leather finisher or beeswax.
For burnishing the cut edges to a smooth finish.
Step 1, cut to length and shape the tip

Cut the strap to the total length required. At the tip end, mark and cut a rounded or pointed taper to give a clean finish. Cut on a firm surface with the leatherwork knife in single confident strokes; short scraping strokes tear the leather.
Step 2, skive the buckle end
At the buckle end,
skive

the flesh side (back) of the leather for 60 mm from the end. Hold the knife nearly flat and shave off even slices until the leather is approximately half its original thickness at the very end. This allows the fold around the buckle bar to sit flat without a thick lump at the fold.
Step 3, bevel and sand the edges

Run the edge beveller along both long edges of the strap, holding it at about 45 degrees and keeping the pressure consistent. This removes the sharp corner from the cut edge. Follow with 120-grit then 180-grit sandpaper to smooth the edge.
Step 4, burnish the edges

Dampen the cut edges lightly with water or edge finisher, then burnish with the rounded edge of the bone folder using fast back-and-forth strokes. Heat from the friction compresses and seals the fibre. The edge should become smooth, slightly shiny, and firm. Repeat until the edge no longer feels fibrous.
Step 5, punch the holes

Mark the wear holes at the tip end: the centre hole should sit at the standard waist measurement, with two holes at 25 mm intervals either side. Mark a single hole at the buckle end for the pin to pass through. Punch all holes on a hard surface (a cutting mat over a block of wood protects the bench). Clean round holes indicate a sharp punch; ragged edges indicate a punch that needs sharpening.
Step 6, fit the buckle

Thread the buckle end through the buckle loop, pass the pin through the buckle-end hole, and fold the leather back over the bar. Rivet or stitch the folded section: two copper rivets or a saddle-stitch line 10 mm from the fold hold the buckle permanently. A keeper loop cut from a scrap of the same leather, just wide enough to slide onto the belt, holds the tail end neat.
